5 min read
Just now

--

Most teams reach for the expensive option first. This is the order that usually works.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every time I start working with a team on LLMs, the same question shows up within ten minutes: “Should I fine-tune, use RAG, or just write better prompts?” Most teams start in the wrong place. I’ve watched teams spend months on fine-tuning when a stronger prompt would have solved it in an afternoon.
These three approaches are not competing options. They solve different problems. Think of it as fixing your recipe, buying fresher ingredients, or renovating the kitchen. Same meal, wildly different effort.
Three Approaches. Three Different Problems.
Prompt Engineering — changing what you say to the model. No retraining, no extra infrastructure. In practice that means clearer instructions, tighter constraints, better examples, and cleaner task framing. The base model is already capable; the issue is usually control, not capability.
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — giving the model access to your documents at query time so it can look things up before answering. Same employee, but now you hand them the company wiki before they respond. Architecturally, you are separating reasoning from knowledge…
